entry.title: IBM Cloud® Security and Compliance Center Workload Protection for IBM Cloud® VPC and IBM® PowerVS
entry.content:
IBM Cloud® Security and Compliance Center Workload Protection can be used to find and prioritize software vulnerabilities, detect and respond to threats, and manage configurations, permissions, and compliance from source to run. When deploying your architecture in the Cloud, effective protection is crucial for ensuring the health, compliance and early threat detection of your system. 

This feature can be turned on when deploying using the Catalog tiles: IBM® Power Virtual Server with IBM Cloud® VPC landing zone and IBM® Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA®.

Key Features

  •        Real-time visibility: IBM® Power Virtual Server Cloud Security Posture Management. Workload Protection now supports IBM® Power Virtual Server resources as part of its Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) feature. Supported resources includes IBM® Power Virtual Server Workspaces, Instances, Instance Volumes, Networks, Network Security Groups and Public Networks.
  •        Reports: Resource Report generates a CSV that includes Compliance results with the resource detail.

Who is it of interest to?               

IBM Cloud® VPC, IBM® Power Virtual Server and SAP® on IBM® Power Virtual Server Customers 

Benefits

  • Fully automated installation and configuration, add on feature for IBM® Power Virtual Server with IBM Cloud® VPC landing zone and IBM® Power Virtual Server for SAP HANA®,
  • Continuous monitoring of workloads for threats, vulnerabilities, and suspicious behavior.
  • Uses behavioral analysis and threat intelligence to detect attacks in real-time.
  • Simplifies compliance auditing with continuous posture monitoring.
  • Provides reports and evidence for regulatory and internal audits.
  • Centralized dashboard for visibility across multi-cloud and hybrid workloads.
  • Easy management of security policies and incident responses. 

Links

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKB

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKx

 https://ibm.biz/BdnZKR

Featured Image Source: IBM

entry.title: The Final Countdown to SAP S/4HANA: Simplify Your Journey with SDT
entry.content:
With the deadline for SAP ECC extended maintenance approaching, customers are planning their transition to SAP S/4HANA to lower the overall maintenance costs of their business solutions. To secure business support, they must build a compelling business case for a transition strategy that is cost-efficient, minimally disruptive, and tailored to their requirements. 

There are three main paths to transition to SAP S/4HANA: System Conversion, Selective Data Transition (SDT), and New Implementation. Some SAP customers rule out System Conversion due to unacceptable downtime requirements caused by the size of their systems, while others rule out New Implementation because it does not support the migration of historical data—only open items and master data. In these cases, Selective Data Transition offers a practical, tailored and effective approach, enabling customers to move to SAP S/4HANA in a way that meets both technical and business requirements.

Using the SAP Readiness Check, customers can assess whether Selective Data Transition (SDT) is the most suitable approach for their SAP S/4HANA journey, based on several key factors:

  1. Pre-Projects and Prerequisites
    Many SAP S/4HANA transitions require prerequisite projects such as New GL Activation or CVI Implementation. With SDT, these can be integrated into the main project. SDT supports "on-the-fly" solutions such as for CVI conversion, Account-to-Ledger mapping (including document splitting), Chart of Accounts conversion, company code merges or splits, system consolidations, and cost center/profit center restructuring—all delivered within the scope of the project.
  2. Downtime Constraints
    For businesses with strict downtime limitations, SDT offers a shell conversion approach. This enables system upgrades—including simplification item handling and custom code remediation—early in the project. As a result, final cutover is limited to data migration and conversion only, significantly reducing the overall downtime window for the business. Performance optimization opportunities during the project can further shorten the downtime window.
  3. System Size and Data Volume
    Large system sizes—often caused by lack of archiving, redundant data from inactive company codes, or uncontrolled growth—can drive up infrastructure and licensing costs in both on-premise and SAP RISE environments. SDT allows customers to selectively reduce data volume (e.g., by time slicing historical data) based on legal and business retention requirements, helping control future system size and cost.
  4. Business Transformation Requirements
    SDT supports broader business transformation needs, such as financial process changes or structural data harmonization. These changes can be embedded directly into the migration, ensuring the new SAP S/4HANA platform reflects current and future business requirements. Using the Landscape Transformation Validation Framework, customers can decide on the number of test cases that they need to validate data between the ECC and their future SAP S/4HANA system. 

The reasons outlined above are key drivers for why SAP customers choose to transition to SAP S/4HANA using the Selective Data Transition (SDT) approach. Many customers also need to phase their global business transitions—due to specific operational requirements—rather than pursue a full 'Big Bang' system conversion. SDT enables this phased approach by allowing transitions by organizational unit (e.g., company code). Selecting this option requires a detailed rollout plan and dedicated resources from both the customer and their partners to ensure the successful and feasible execution of each rollout go-live. 

How does it work? 

The Mix and Match approach is another SDT option that allows SAP customers to begin with a new SAP S/4HANA system, selectively migrating customizations, developments, and only clean data—historical data included if required.”

How does it work? 

The transition to SAP S/4HANA should not be perceived as a technical deadline, but as a strategic opportunity to provide SAP customers with a stronger, future-ready platform that drives end-to-end business transformation and excellence. 

Delivering a Selective Data Transition (SDT) project gives SAP customers the opportunity to implement key business changes as part of the required data migration/conversion, ensuring their future platform is aligned with their strategic business goals.

For support or to discuss your specific business requirements, please don’t hesitate to reach out or contact DMLT directly via their global inbox sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com .

entry.title: SAP Fiori tools 2506 update – Open source, faster app creation, adaptation boost, AI enhancements
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the 2506 release of SAP Fiori tools, bringing enhancements that make full-stack development with SAP Fiori elements and RAP even more seamless—from initial app creation to UI adaptation and low-code configuration. Whether you're just starting your project or looking to fine-tune a production app, this update continues our focus on productivity, extensibility, and user experience.

Let’s take a closer look at what’s new in this release.

SAP Fiori generator: Now fully open source

After a long journey of gradual transition, the SAP Fiori Application Generator is now fully open source. This means all the core logic that powers the generator has been moved to a public repository, giving you full transparency and the opportunity to contribute directly.

If you’re curious about how it works under the hood or want to propose improvements, now is the perfect time to get involved. Learn more in this dedicated blog post.

💡Tip: If you rely on custom generators or extensions, this is a great opportunity to align your tools more closely with the open-source foundation.

Accelerating RAP development with Quick Fiori App

Deploy a ready to use SAP Fiori elements app with ADT, and import it into your dev environment with SAP Fiori tools if tweaks are needed.

To simplify the initial stages of app development, you can now quickly generate a working SAP Fiori elements application based on a RAP business object—directly from ADT. With just a few clicks, a basic app is deployed using the service's annotations, giving you a functional starting point in record time. And when you’re ready to go further, you can import the app into SAP Business Application Studio or Visual Studio Code using SAP Fiori tools, continue customizing, and redeploy as usual.

This feature is available starting with SAP BTP ABAP Environment 2505 and SAP Fiori tools version 1.17.6.

Enhancing adaptation projects with local annotations and more

Use quick actions to open the right adaptation dialog for your task.

UI adaptation projects continue to grow more powerful. This release introduces support for changes that typically involve modifications to the manifest.json—such as creating new pages—now available directly via quick actions in the adaptation editor.

We also added support for creating local annotation files within adaptation projects. Whether you're defining new views or tweaking behavior, you now benefit from the same intelligent editing experience provided by the Language Server Protocol (LSP), including auto-completion, code snippets, and quick fixes—making it easier than ever to stay within the Fiori elements design guidelines.

The outline tree and property panel have also received a usability upgrade, with more quick actions and context menu options—particularly for working within tables.

Smarter tooling and continuous enhancements with AI

The SAP Fiori generator now defaults to using virtual endpoints in newly created projects, simplifying preview configuration and aligning better with modern dev environments.

The AI-powered Project Accelerator continues to evolve as well. It now supports Word documents containing both text and images, giving you even more flexibility when starting a project from design inputs. You can also decide exactly where the generated project should be placed in your workspace, putting you in full control of your file structure.

In the Application Modeler, our low-code editor for SAP Fiori elements, we’ve expanded the configuration options for micro charts. You can now define sort criteria for chart columns and use additional chart types, including “Column,” “Comparison,” and “Harvey.” These changes make it easier to present complex data visually with minimal effort.

Finally, when deploying to Cloud Foundry, you now have the option to select “Application Frontend” deployment within SAP Fiori tools, streamlining the deployment flow even further.

Looking ahead

The future of SAP Fiori tools continues to be shaped by your feedback and evolving development needs. We're working on:

  • Bringing new capabilities to the Project Accelerator, including the ability to extract app information from PDFs, and enhanced Application Modeler’s editing capabilities.
  • Deeper alignment between RAP and SAP Fiori development to further streamline the full-stack development workflow.
  • Better in-tool troubleshooting during development, helping you catch issues earlier.
  • Extended support for SAP Fiori elements features in the Application Modeler for a more complete low-code experience.

As always, we welcome your input and questions in the SAP Fiori elements and tools Community.

We hope these updates help you build great apps faster and more efficiently. Let us know what you think!

If you want to learn about updates to our SAP Fiori tools and technologies, please sign up for the SAP Fiori development newsletter.

entry.title: Task Center ECC Connector – Bridging the Gap During Your S/4HANA Transition
entry.content:
Are ECC-based workflows causing bottlenecks in your S/4HANA migration journey? You're not without a solution.

Introducing the ECC Connector for Task Center, delivered in partnership with MHP, a valuable enabler for customers navigating hybrid SAP landscapes. Whether you're mid-migration or working in a dual-system environment, this connector helps integrate your classic ECC tasks directly into the modern SAP Task Center experience on BTP.

What Is the ECC Connector?

The ECC Connector is a purpose-built solution designed to seamlessly pull tasks from ECC systems into SAP Task Center—your central inbox for all workflow items across SAP and third-party systems.

Built on SAP BTP CAP architecture and deployed directly into your SAP and BTP infrastructure, the connector supports:

  • Pulling ECC-based approval tasks

  • Operating through My Inbox actions (including bulk operations)

  • Detailed task data including attachments and notifications

  • Backend extensibility for customer-specific enhancements

What’s Delivered as an ECC connector?

As part of the implementation, MHP provides:

  • BTP CAP API Interface deployed on your SAP BTP instance

  • Backend Interface in the MHP namespace for ECC/SAP on-premise systems

  • Full implementation support

  • Tailored adjustments to your business requirements

Scope & Eligibility

Your organization qualifies if:

  • You have a BTP subaccount with Task Center and Backend Connectivity enabled (via Cloud Connector & Principal Propagation)

  • You're running My Inbox on ECC for existing approval processes

  • Your SAP system meets minimum version: SAP_BASIS, SAP_ABA, SAP_GWFND ≥ 750

Benefits and Roadmap

Current Capabilities Include:

  • Pulling tasks from ECC backend

  • Support for My Inbox actions (approve/reject)

  • Attachments, details, and multi-backend support

  • Easy display integration for My Inbox detail views

What's Next:

  • Adjustments based on early customer feedback

  • Enhanced support for multiple backend systems, clients, and 3rd party SaaS

  • Full SaaS readiness with 24/7 support, SAP Store listing, and standard rollout models

Why ECC Connector is crutial during S/4HANA Migration?

During your migration to S/4HANA, it's not uncommon to maintain hybrid landscapes where legacy ECC processes coexist with modern SAP BTP components. Instead of re-building workflows or forcing users into multiple inboxes, the ECC Connector provides a unified user experience, reduces disruption, and supports a smoother transition.

ECC Connector in action

<Coming Soon>

Curious how this can work in your landscape?

If you're in the midst of an S/4HANA transition and struggling with disconnected ECC workflows, the ECC Connector for Task Center could be your answer. Reach out to SAP or our partner MHP to join the program and bring clarity and cohesion to your task management.

entry.title: SAP User Feedback Day 2025: Putting Customers and end users at the Center of AI-driven Innovation
entry.content:
Delivering exceptional User Experience is the result of an ever-evolving journey fueled by insights gained from our customers and end users. SAP User Feedback Day 2025 embodied our commitment to listen, learn, and collaboratively shape a future where AI plays a pivotal role in redefining work processes.

AI: The Driving Force Behind Innovation

In today’s fast-paced world, AI is revolutionizing the way we operate—transforming learned patterns and optimizing workflows while empowering humans as the ultimate decision-makers. SAP User Feedback Day embraced this theme, inviting customers and end users to engage in AI-focused dialogues and understand its impact on real-world applications within our products.

On June 17 + 18, 2025, in Walldorf, Germany, SAP brought together end users from seven European customers to explore the transformative power of AI in industries ranging from Automotive to Life Sciences and Private Equity. Our focus was on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, showcasing diverse use cases powered by AI:

  • Fiori First: Manage Sales Documents
  • Batch Release Hub Digital Assistant
  • Joule as a Project Assistant (for Non-Professional Services industry)
  • AI-driven resilient supply chain (IBP)
  • Production Order Release Agent
  • AI Error Resolution

 

Throughout the six specialized use cases and a total of 15 hours of user research being conducted in just the first day, users and SAP experts delved into AI-powered scenarios highlighting AI's role in enhancing productivity and decision-making processes while ensuring human oversight. Employing diverse methods like interviews, usability testing, and moderated discussions, the business users were able to explore how AI can support them in their everyday tasks and provide meaningful feedback crucial for future product iterations.

In addition to the User Research, business users had the opportunity to explore six booths to dive deeper into what AI can do for them and how their feedback shapes SAP’s products.

 

Building a Shared Vision for the Future

SAP’s objective remains the same: empower users with efficient AI tools that enrich their experience and facilitate success. The insights gathered directly influence the alignment of our products with evolving expectations. Engaging face-to-face with end users is an invaluable experience, reflecting our collective journey to tackle today’s challenges and anticipate tomorrow’s solutions. The customers and their end users explored AI's future potential through innovation workshops on the second day of the event, igniting anticipation for forthcoming developments.

The first workshop, "Shape the Next SAP Experience with GenUI," invited key users and business process experts to delve into the next generation of SAP experiences. Participants explored challenges and opportunities within SAP products, offering valuable feedback on GenUI's potential impacts on their roles and organizations. Engaging in brainstorming, foresight exercises, and collaborative discussions, attendees contributed to defining the future of SAP's user experiences.

The second workshop, "Co-creating the Future with AI: AI in Your Company," provided IT experts, in-house consultants, and business process specialists a platform to explore AI's transformative potential in their businesses. This interactive session encouraged participants to share their current AI experiences and identify key opportunities where AI could address pressing challenges. Through collaborative brainstorming, attendees envisioned future states and crafted a "vision journey," discussing actionable steps needed to prepare for this AI-driven future. Both workshops were instrumental in harnessing collective intelligence to drive innovation and enhance user experiences.

Listening and collaborating with our users, we gained many invaluable insights on which we now can start to act. SAP User Feedback Day once again proved our commitment to customers is never fleeting—we pledge ongoing engagement to translate feedback into actionable improvements. The insights drive tangible benefits, refining our products to meet and exceed user aspirations.

Customer Voices

“It was a very good experience to be here. It was a big chance to have the possibility of influencing the products which are not already on the road. It is good to see that SAP values our input. Basically we are partners, we are committed to SAP solutions, we are very satisfied with the performance right know and the better SAP gets, the better we are getting.”

“It was really nice, thank you for inviting us. Really great insights, I learned a lot and got to know lots of people. I’ve seen a lot of features I’m very exciting about – this is the future.”

Collective insights steer our path as we explore AI's immense potential together. SAP remains dedicated to leveraging AI’s capabilities while prioritizing human expertise, crafting products that deliver unmatched value and satisfaction. Join us on this transformative journey toward the future of AI-driven innovation!

entry.title: Introducing the New RISE with SAP Methodology Dashboard: Operations View
entry.content:
What is the Operations View?

The Operations View is a dashboard available for RISE with SAP customers and is designed to deliver daily insights and historical data on system health-related key performance indicators, enabling organizations to efficiently align with the clean core compliance goals of the operations dimension. 

Several metrics are collected from SAP Cloud ALM applications for all your configured and productive SAP S/4HANA systems. These are then evaluated on a daily basis against specific thresholds. The results of these metric compliance calculations are aggregated and organized into KPIs and scores, which can be viewed on the overview and detailed pages of the dashboard.

The metrics, thresholds, and evaluation algorithms are shared to ensure a homogeneous and consistent compliance calculation across all RISE with SAP customers.

 

Key Features and Benefits

The Operations View dashboard offers daily insights and includes up to 30 days of historical data. The data in the dashboard displays the most recent values from yesterday and covers the System Health score and its key performance indicators (KPIs). It offers the following key features and benefits:

  • Overview of the System Health score for all your productive SAP S/4HANA systems (Cloud Private Edition systems and On Premise systems)
  • A system-specific view to identify and analyze metric deviations responsible for each KPI's calculation
  • Jump-ins to standard SAP Cloud ALM applications to access the highest level of details

 

          

 

Concept and Data Model

At the core of the Operations View dashboard stands the definition of Scores, KPIs, and Metrics:

  • Scores
    • A score represents a percentage that describes how well a customer aligns with a specific strategic goal within the operations dimension definition. Examples include ensuring that customer systems maintain good health or verifying that applications belonging to SAP Cloud ALM for Operations are properly set up.
  • KPIs
    • KPIs are distinct objectives that contribute to the strategic goals represented by the score.
    • KPIs are calculated based on the compliance level of each metric.
  • Metrics
    • A metric is a numerical value that offers insight into performance or status. It's often linked to a specific timestamp, such as the number of ABAP dumps (Runtime Errors) from yesterday. 
    • The metrics in this dashboard are retrieved from the Health Monitoring and Integrations & Exceptions Monitoring apps of SAP Cloud ALM.

 

 

All scores (equal to strategic goals) contribute to the Operations Index, an overall percentage that defines the level of a customer's alignment with the clean core compliance goals for the operations dimension.

An Operations Index of 100% indicates a full compliance with the current recommended clean core best practices for the operations dimension. Note that best practices may be enriched and enhanced in future versions by taking into account customer feedback and additional developments.

 

Compliance and Propagation

The Operations View dashboard collects various metrics and calculates a compliance level for each one of them. The result of these calculations are then propagated by aggregating the values into different KPIs and scores. Hence, the metric compliance is at the core of all the displayed values in the dashboard.

How is a metric compliance calculated?

For each metric, we have defined an associated SLA (threshold). The Metric Compliance Calculation is essentially a measure of the distance between an SLA and a Metric value. The result of this difference is then passed to a specific function that returns a percentage value between -100% (minimum level of compliance) and +100% (maximum level of compliance).

 

 

 

 

The dashboard collects daily metrics and calculates the compliance for each SAP S/4HANA productive system*.

Given that the metrics vary in importance for the KPI calculations, each KPI is derived from a weighted average of its contributing metric compliances.

 

A similar approach is used for the calculation of the scores and the Operations Index: each score (strategic goal) is calculated from a weighted average of its KPIs. The overall Operations Index is a composition of all these scores.

 

*The metrics in the Health Monitoring app (SAP Cloud ALM) are related to specific clients. The Operations View dashboard considers only productive clients for its calculations.

 

Current State: System Health, KPIs, and Metrics

The System Health is the first score provided with the release of the Operations View dashboard. It's designed to help customers keep their business-critical systems agile and in good health.

The System Health score is calculated based on the following four KPIs: 

  • Connectivity
  • Exceptions
  • Background Processing
  • Performance

 

To view all scores, KPIs, and metrics that are available in the Operations View dashboard, and to check how much each KPI and metric contributes to the respective value calculations, see At a Glance: Available Scores, KPIs, and Metrics on the SAP Help Portal.

Don't forget that the in-app help can also be used as a source to get more details about the Operations View dashboard and its content.

As mentioned earlier, the dashboard calculates the health of each system. You can view the overall system health (for you selected systems) on the overview page, along with details for each system in the Eligible Systems section.

By clicking on the header of the system tiles, you can access the Operation Details view. Here, a selected system's details along with the metrics responsible for each KPI calculation are shown.

In the Metrics section of the Operation Details view, you can see a list of contributing metrics displayed as charts and cards. These show the latest values from yesterday.

By clicking on a metric card, the dashboard directs you to the standard SAP Cloud ALM applications: Health Monitoring app or Integration & Exception Monitoring app, depending on the metric type.

 

How to Use the Operations View Dashboard?

Please note: it's perfectly acceptable for the overall system health score to be below 100%.

The dashboard acts as a daily report, showing how well customers' systems comply with the clean core principles for the operations dimension. Remember that these principles are expressed through KPIs, metrics, weights, and SLAs used in the respective calculations. Over time, we'll improve these elements.

Values lower than 85% are marked as Warning while values below 70% are marked as Critical. These ratings indicate that there may be anomalous situations with the involved metrics that need checking. This includes potentially missing configurations.

A low System Health score usually results from one or more low KPIs. If you identify a low score on the Overview page, start by identifying the system(s) responsible for the low value. The overall System Health score is simply the average of the individual System Health scores.

Within each system, you can easily identify which KPI is responsible for a low score. To access the Operation Details view, click on the header of a system tile under the Eligible Systems section. This detailed view provides a list of metrics used for the KPI calculations.

Keep in mind that some KPIs have a greater influence on score calculations due to their specific weights (see At a Glance: Available Scores, KPIs, and Metrics on the SAP Help Portal for all details). Higher weights are assigned to KPIs that are typically considered more important for evaluation in the "clean core operations" context. These KPIs also offer customers more opportunities to influence the metric values in the dashboard.

Let's have a look at the following example: the Performance KPI has a weight of only 10% in the System Health score calculation. Additionally, the KPI is based on two metrics with their own weights for the KPI calculation: Average Response Time (90%) and Maximum Response Time (10%). These metrics have fairly high SLAs: 4 seconds for the Average Response Time and 200 seconds for the Maximum Response Time. In contrast, the Exceptions KPI has a weight of 25%. Exceptions, such as ABAP Runtime Errors, can generally be addressed by tuning the system configuration, fixing code for custom applications, and ensuring that programs run smoothly.

To improve the value of a KPI, ensure the corresponding metrics fall below the SLAs defined for each metric in the At a Glance: Available Scores, KPIs, and Metrics topic on the SAP Help Portal. The SAP Cloud ALM for Operations applications, such as Health Monitoring, Integration & Exception Monitoring, and Job & Automation Monitoring apps, are invaluable tools. They help you identify which instances have connectivity issues, which runtime error categories occur most frequently and for which programs, and which jobs fail most often.

 

Next Steps: How to Get Started?

Step 1: Ask your administrator to assign the role Operations Dashboard Viewer to your user. The easiest way to do this is to use the User Management application in SAP Cloud ALM and scroll down to the RISE with SAP section when editing the role assignments of a user:

Note that you'll be able to see the RISE with SAP section only if you are a RISE with SAP customer.

Step 2: Make sure that the Health Monitoring and Integration & Exception Monitoring apps are configured for your SAP S/4HANA systems. Also, don't forget to activate the following categories in the Integration & Exception Monitoring app: ABAP Runtime, ABAP System Log, and ABAP Application Log. For a more detailed explanation, check out the following page: How to connect SAP S/4HANA and SAP Business Suite 7 on-premise systems to SAP Cloud ALM

Step 3: Confirm that you have at least the following ST-PI add-on version installed on your SAP S/4HANA systems: ST-PI 740 SP28.

For a detailed description of all prerequisites, see Prerequisites on the SAP Help Portal.

 

FAQ

Is this a monitoring tool?

  • No, this tool provides daily reports on RISE with SAP customers' clean core progress within the operations dimension (based on SAP's evaluation).

My System Health score is below 100%. Is this bad?

  • Ideally, the System Health score should be rated as green, meaning it has a value between 85% and 100%. The goal isn't necessarily to achieve a score of 100%. Instead, use the dashboard as a tool to identify any anomalous behavior in certain operational areas of your systems.

Which products are supported?

  • The dashboard supports SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems only.

Why is it not possible see and select my development systems?

  • The compliances, KPIs, and scores are calculated for your productive systems only. Within a productive system only the client marked as "productive" is considered in this dashboard.

Why does my system always show an overall health of 40% even when nothing is configured?

  • In the current state, a default compliance value is assigned when the dashboard receives no metrics due to missing configurations. For the Performance and Connectivity KPIs, the default value is zero. For the Exceptions and Background Processing KPIs, the default compliance is 100%. When both the Health Monitoring and Integration & Exceptions Monitoring apps aren't configured, the final result is 40%. This is due to the respective KPI Weights in the score calculations.
  • Here is an example calculation: 50% × 0% (Connectivity) + 25% × 100% (Exceptions) + 15% × 100% (Background Processing) + 10% × 0% (Performance) = 40%
  • A fixed System Health score of 40% is a pretty good indication that there is an issue with the configuration of the Health Monitoring or the Integration & Exception Monitoring apps.
  • Note that this behavior will change in the future by taking into account the actual status of the configuration.

Under which component can I report incidents?

  • Please use the following component for reporting incidents: SV-CLM-RMD

I can see my SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition systems but not my SAP S/4HANA (On Premise) ones. What should I do?

  • This issue is likely due to the missing value in the Customer ID field in the Landscape Management app. Check the prerequisite section described in the SAP Help Portal for further details: Prerequisites

I'm a RISE with SAP customer, but I can't see the dashboard. What should I do?

  • In certain situations, depending on the type of contract (standard vs. tailored), there may be an issue with correctly identifying customer contracts. If you're sure that you're a RISE with SAP customer but you can't see your systems, we recommend opening an incident under the following component: SV-CLM-RMD.

Can I configure my own SLAs?

  • No, you cannot. The current thresholds are configured to be the same for all RISE with SAP customers.

How were the SLAs selected?

  • It depends. For some metrics, the SLAs are chosen based on current best practices. For other metrics, like used for the  calculation of the Exceptions KPI, the thresholds were determined by conducting a statistical analysis on real productive SAP S/4HANA systems after removing significant outliers.

Where can I see the overall Operations Index?

  • The current version of the dashboard includes only one score: System Health. Since the Operations Index is calculated based on various scores, it corresponds to the System Health score in the current state of the dashboard.

 

Want to learn more about how you can leverage this powerful tool for your SAP S/4HANA systems? Visit the SAP Help Portal to access detailed resources on the RISE with SAP Methodology Operations View dashboard. There, you’ll find step-by-step guides, prerequisites and best practices to help you get the most out of this innovative tool.

entry.title: New enhancements to RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard
entry.content:
We are excited to announce significant enhancements to the RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard, building upon the application's successful release in January this year. These new updates aim to provide a more comprehensive and insightful experience, empowering you to optimize your SAP S/4HANA system and achieving a clean core. 

**Improved Software Stack Assessment** 

The updated dashboard now offers a more robust software stack assessment, emphasizing the importance of maintaining the latest SAP S/4HANA product version or feature package stack. By ensuring your system is up-to-date, you can leverage SAP-delivered innovations, mitigate security risks, and align with the clean core principles. This enhancement enables you to:  

  • Verify that your system is on the latest SAP S/4HANA product version or feature package stack 
  • Install the latest feature package stack/support package stack to stay current with SAP innovations and security patches 

**Simplified Upgrade Readiness Check** 

Upgrading to SAP S/4HANA has never been more streamlined, thanks to the SAP Readiness Check for SAP S/4HANA Upgrades. This innovative tool empowers you to assess your current system's readiness for a seamless upgrade to your desired target SAP S/4HANA release. With a single glance, the SAP Readiness Check card provides a comprehensive overview of the most critical findings from your system's latest assessment, enabling you to gauge your preparedness for the upgrade or estimate the necessary effort. 

**Enhanced Extensibility Information** 

To facilitate a better understanding of your system's extensibility, we have introduced enhanced information on separate software components using the ABAP language version ABAP for Cloud Development. This update helps you: 

  • Determine if a separate software component has been created for building extensions in Tier 1 
  • Gain insight into your system's extensibility architecture and identify potential areas for improvement 

**Unused Customer Objects: A Key Focus Area** 

The updated dashboard now provides a dedicated card to track Unused Customer Objects, which are extensions created outside of the SAP standard. Reducing the number of Unused Customer Objects is a crucial goal of the extensibility dimension. The card displays: 

  • The current share of Unused Customer Objects in your system 
  • The number of Unused Objects and Trackable Objects in your system 

**Execution of Customer Objects: A Measure of Business Process Reliance** 

To assess your efforts for future upgrades and adaptation, the dashboard now offers a card that measures the Execution of Customer Objects. This update helps you: 

  • Understand how heavily your business processes rely on executable customer objects 
  • Compare the frequency of executable customer object execution to the total execution of all executable objects in your system (including SAP, partner, and customer objects) 

**Introducing the Operations View** 

In addition to these enhancements, we are introducing a new view.  

The RISE with SAP Operations View is a dashboard available for RISE with SAP customers designed to deliver daily insights and historical data on system health-related key performance indicators, enabling organizations to efficiently align with the clean core compliance goals from the operations dimension. 

Several metrics are collected from SAP Cloud ALM applications for all your configured, productive  SAP S/4HANA systems and evaluated on a daily basis against specific thresholds. The results of these metric compliance calculations are aggregated and organized in KPIs and scores displayed in the overview and detailed pages.  

Metrics, thresholds and evaluation algorithms are shared ensuring a homogeneous compliance calculation across all RISE with SAP customers. 

***Key Features and Benefits*** 

The Operations View provides daily insights, featuring up to 30 days of historical data available until the day before, for the System Health score and its KPIs: Connectivity, Exceptions, Background Processing and Performance. 

  • Overview of System Health score for all your productive SAP S/4HANA systems (Cloud Private Edition and On Premise) 
  • System-specific view to identify and analyze metrics deviations responsible for each KPI 
  • Jump-in to standard SAP Cloud ALM applications to access the highest level of details 

Overview page of the Operations View Dashboard 

For more information about the Operations View dashboard, see https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/operations-view on the SAP Help Portal and the “Introducing the new RISE with SAP Operations View Dashboard” on the SAP Community Blogs portal. 

These updates demonstrate our ongoing commitment to delivering a comprehensive and actionable RISE with SAP Methodology dashboard. By leveraging these enhancements, you can further optimize your SAP S/4HANA system, achieve a clean core, and drive business success. More details can be found on our Help Portal page: https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-alm/applicationhelp/rise-with-sap. 

If you don’t want to miss out any new feature, subscribe to the What’s New viewer:  What's New in SAP Cloud ALM | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: One of SAP TM's hidden gems: The Load Utilization Determination Component
entry.content:
I've found that in SAP Transportation Management, some very useful components don't always get the attention they deserve. This clearly applies to the Load Utilization Determination component, and this short blog post intends to rectify this somewhat.

What It Does

Simply put, this component answers an important question: "What transportation resources will I need for this set of products?" It does this by combining Package Building and Load Consolidation capabilities to run a simulation for a set of products and resources. The component doesn't actually create shipments or change your data - it just shows you what would be needed to move your products. The following result screenshot of test report /SCMTMS/TEST_PLN_LUD illustrates this, by listing the resources with utilization percentages for the packages (pallets) which were placed by the load consolidation components. The pallets themselves where created for the products using the unified package building component:

Practical Applications and Business Value

This tool helps in several business situations:

  • Resource Forecasting: Planning your transportation needs for upcoming sales periods based on expected orders, helping you secure the right carriers and vehicles ahead of time.

  • Bid Preparation/Sales order processing: When responding to customer requests, you can quickly estimate what transportation you'll need, allowing for more accurate cost estimates.

  • Scenario Planning: Test different approaches by seeing how changes in packaging or routing might affect your resource needs.

An example might look like this: A electronics distributor receives a rush order: 200 laptops, 150 monitors, and 300 accessories going to Walldorf. Before committing to same-day delivery, they run LUD:

  • Input: Product quantities + available truck types
  • Output: "You'll need 2 standard trucks at 85% capacity, or 1 large truck at 60% capacity"
  • Decision: Quote customer for large truck option with room for additional orders.

Getting Started

Setting up this component requires some basic configuration work with your planning profile (including load consolidation and unified package building settings) and master data. Note 3618303 provides basic information on how to consume this component in your custom project. The component is available for TM 9.6 and S/4HANA 1809 onwards.

Other options

Consulting solution

If a custom implementation is not an option for you, you could also take a look at the consulting solution for Transport Load Consolidation, which has implemented a process that consumes this component and integrates into sales order and delivery processes. Please refer to note 3045005 for further information on this solution.

Standard solution

As I mentioned above, we currently have no standard solution that embeds this functionality in a TM process. We do have plans in that direction, so if you have requirements or feedback around this we would love to hear from you.

entry.title: Your opportunity to Influence SAP – Customer Engagement Initiative Projects 2025-2 for SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
An exciting opportunity to influence SAP has started with the first cycle of the 2025 SAP Customer Engagement Initiative (CEI). The SAP Customer Engagement Initiative enables you as an SAP customer or partner to get early insights into SAP’s product developments and directly work with the developers to define and shape future product directions.

We are offering several interesting opportunities for customers to influence SAP’s S/4HANA solutions.

Find here the list of all projects that are relevant for the SAP S4/HANA Community or browse the whole project list here.

The projects will be open for customer registration until July 25, 2025.


SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud projects for registration:

 

ERP Cloud Delivery & Excellence

Potential Workforce Person Data Correction Tool

Test Data Variants–Driven Test Process Flow Control and Execution

Tool-Agnostic Test Automation from AI-Generated Process Variants for ERP Systems

 

ERP Digital Supply Chain Cloud

Empowering Reliability: Data-Driven Maintenance Efficiency Analysis for Strategy Optimization

Freight Rate Management for transportation processes with a low to medium complexity

Reimagine Warehouse Management with Joule

 

ERP Finance & Risk

AI assisted creation of balance validation rules AND comment proposal

Financial Reporting according to IFRS18 in S4HANA Cloud

Financial Statements in the New Review Booklets Analytical Application

 

ERP Private Cloud

Best Practice for Inspection Checklists in SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud

 

ERP Public Cloud

Actual Liquidity Analysis

AI Agent for Open Item Clearing

AI-powered chatbot (Joule) for Intelligent Margin Analysis

Assess and improve your Quote-to-Cash process with meaningful Business Health KPIs

Enhancing Payment Run Experience

Optimizing Partner Solution Support in the SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Ecosystem

Price Recommendation in S/4HANA Public Cloud Edition

Research: Payment Advice In Existing Business Process

Sales Contract Management - Artificial Intelligent Handling for Quantity/Value Consumption by Joule

SAP Business Suite: Agility and Innovation through seamless Integration and Harmonization

Successor for Create and Process Non-Assigned Handling Units (HU02)

 

ERP User Experience

AI-assisted error resolution and data entry for SAP Fiori apps

AI-assisted Smart Visualization of MyHome for To-Dos (situations)

 

How do I register on the Customer Influence website? A short overview about how to find and register for a CEI project can be found on influence.sap.com.

Follow-up after registration: When you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call with the SAP project lead. At this point further participation is optional. Typically, all activities are governed by the Feedback Agreement with SAP.

Thank you in advance for your participation!

entry.title: New SAP Build Process Automation content for side-by-side extensions with flexible workflows
entry.content:
My colleagues published a SAP Build Process Automation action package "Flexible Workflow Scenario Notifications Action" on SAP Business Accelerator Hub and in the SAP Build Store so that it becomes easier for Citizen Developer to discover and consume it.

It oﬀers an action for an outcome driven extension workﬂow and makes it easier to combine ﬂexible workﬂows from SAP S/4HANA with SAP Build Process Automation capabilities.

From a use case perspective, you could think of including process participants from outside (like external project managers) into the SAP S/4HANA Process. Or you include centrally managed services from SAP BTP like a Rules Service or Joule Skills into your SAP S/4HANA workﬂow project. Or you like to leverage ML services like the recommendation capability of SAP Task Center, which is only available for SAP Build Process Automation tasks? For the blog I took the use case with process participants from outside to explain the possibilities.

In line with side-by-side extensibility ﬂexible workﬂow oﬀers an activitytype "extension step" which can be implemented by flexible workflow scenarios to offer side-by-side extension. It is guideline for all scenario developers to enable it.

I will give a short overview and add some points in addition to the oﬃcial documentation.

The solution architecture gives an overview on the connection between Sales Order Creation in SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP. Over the ﬂexible workﬂow capability of SAP Business Workﬂow it is possible to start an extension process and get back the result / outcome from SAP Build Process Automation .

Configuration in SAP S/4HANA starts with definition of decision reasons for sales orders. In our use case we want to start a flexible workﬂow which is the internal approval solution. After setting an internal approval reason a BAdI checks after saving the Sales Order if an approval is necessary. If true a start event is thrown and checks if a workﬂow is deﬁned in the Manage Workﬂow Application for our scenario. If the scenario contains an extension step, the business process expert is able to configures an extension step with a process deﬁnition from SAP Build Process Automation from the connected BTP subaccount. During runtime when the flow comes to the extension, a SAP Build Process Automation workflow is started via api and a standardize payload. 

For shared environments an API Trigger is needed. In the extension workﬂow of our use case we derive the complete sales order header data over an API call to SAP S/4HANA and use this data for Agent Determination. After agent determination via decision table a user decision is modelled with two outcomes.

After the user decision took place we need to set the outcome via script and use it in the action content package. Go to SAP Build Store. Search for "flexible".

Click on the action project for review the details.

Add the action to your project.

To use the action and point to the correct ABAP-Backend-System:

  1. define a destination variable in the SAP Build Process Automation project.
  2. define a destination in the BTP Cockpit to the ABAP-Backend-System(s)
  3. map the destination to the destination variable during deployment of the project.

Define the workflowInstanceId in the context and fill it from the start payload. For the names of the outcomes you need to look up the flexible workflow scenario or check the documentation of the used scenario. If it is custom build in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, private edition or SAP S/4HANA (like in this blog) check your implementation of the extension step and fill the outcomes accordingly.

Please be aware that the development livecycle of the projects on SAP S/4HANA and SAP BTP is independent. We work on further enhancements to simplify the integration via the extensibility wizard and also on more features to enrich the possibilities. So stay tuned. A big thanks to all colleagues supporting the integration.

entry.title: I Built LangChain-lite for ABAP, and Solved the Enterprise AI Cold Start Problem
entry.content:
Why enterprise AI projects fail at the "last mile"

AGI has been achieved!* Now your ABAP-Native AI can reach enterprise data where it lives.

Hello! 👋 It's Alice again, and I've spent the last 1.5 years building what I wish had existed when I started my AI journey: a complete LLM orchestration framework that lives entirely within SAP.

TL;DR:

  • GitHub: oisee/zllm (releasing incrementally - link in first comment)
  • Problem: Enterprise AI projects die in "integration hell" between proof-of-concept and production
  • Solution: Native ABAP AI orchestration - no Python, no APIs, no middleware
  • Result: Build production AI workflows in hours, not months

Remember my previous posts?

  • 🔍ZVDB brought vector search to ABAP

  • 📏PREDICTOKEN eliminated expensive tokenizer calls

  • 🔗The OData-MCP bridge connected 15,000+ enterprise services to AI

 

Those were puzzle pieces; ZLLM is the complete picture.

The "Last Mile" Problem Nobody Talks About

Your AI proof-of-concept is brilliant. The CEO is excited. Then someone asks:

"When can we connect this to our actual SAP system?"

Traditional Answer: "We need APIs, data pipelines, authentication, environments, deployments, monitoring..."

Six months later: Still in integration hell. 💀

ZLLM Answer: "It's already connected." ✨

What Enterprise AI Actually Looks Like

This is what you can build today, in pure ABAP, with zero external dependencies:

" Complete customer intelligence pipeline
DATA(lo_flow) = zcl_llm_00_flow_lazy=>new( VALUE #(
  ( lo_extract_customer_data )    
  ( lo_analyze_behavior_patterns )
  ( lo_identify_upsell_opportunities )
  ( lo_generate_action_plan )     
  ( lo_update_crm_notes )         
) ).

DATA(lr_result) = lo_flow->exec( REF #( lv_customer_id ) ).

Time to production: Hours External systems involved: Zero** Integration code: Zero

The Template Engine That Understands Your Data

Here's where ZLLM gets magical - watch this:

" Your nested ABAP structure
DATA: BEGIN OF ls_order,
        customer TYPE BEGIN OF customer_struct,
          name TYPE string,
          vip_status TYPE abap_bool,
        END OF customer,
        items TYPE TABLE OF order_item,
      END OF ls_order.

" Template that just works
DATA(lo_pattern) = zcl_llm_00_pat=>new(
  'Customer {T-CUSTOMER-NAME} (VIP: {T-CUSTOMER-VIP_STATUS}) ' &&
  'ordered {T-ITEMS-PRODUCT_NAME} for ${T-ITEMS-PRICE}'
).

DATA(lv_prompt) = lo_pattern->apply( REF #( ls_order ) ).

The template engine automatically:

 

  • ✅Navigates deep structure hierarchies
  • ✅Iterates over internal tables
  • ✅Converts complex data to JSON when needed
  • ✅Handles NULL values gracefully

 

No JSON glue code. No data mapping. No field-by-field extraction. It just works.

 

Lazy Execution: The Enterprise Performance Secret

Traditional AI workflows block everything:

# Traditional synchronous hell
result1 = llm.call(prompt1)          # Wait 3 seconds
result2 = llm.call(prompt2)          # Wait 3 seconds 
result3 = llm.call(prompt3)          # Wait 3 seconds
# Total: 9 seconds of blocking execution

ZLLM uses lazy execution for real performance:

" Start all operations immediately (non-blocking)
DATA(lo_result1) = lo_step1->start( ir_input ).
DATA(lo_result2) = lo_step2->start( ir_input ).
DATA(lo_result3) = lo_step3->start( ir_input ).

" Collect results when ready
DATA(lr_final1) = lo_result1->collect( ).  " ~3 seconds total

 

The Security Story Enterprise Can Actually Use

ZLLM includes security foundations that scale to enterprise requirements:

At Rest:

" Default: Symmetric XOR with seed (fine for PoC/development)
SET PARAMETER ID 'ZLLM_CODEC' FIELD '12345'.

" Production: Implement your codec interface
DATA(lo_enterprise_codec) = zcl_your_enterprise_codec=>new( ).

At Runtime:

" Inherits SAP's proven security model
" If user can't access data in GUI, they can't access via AI
" No new security surface area

Bottom Line: ZLLM starts secure enough for development and PoCs, with clear paths to enterprise-grade security when you need it.

Real Demo: 30-Second Customer Intelligence

METHOD generate_customer_intelligence.
  DATA(lo_extract) = zcl_llm_00_step_lazy=>new_from_string(
    iv_usr = 'Extract key purchasing patterns from: {T}'
    iv_sys = 'Return as structured JSON with trends'
    io_llm = lo_llm
  ).

  DATA(lo_opportunities) = zcl_llm_00_step_lazy=>new_from_string(
    iv_usr = 'Based on patterns {T}, identify 3 upsell opportunities'
    io_llm = lo_llm
  ).

  DATA(lo_flow) = zcl_llm_00_flow_lazy=>new( VALUE #(
    ( lo_extract ) ( lo_opportunities )
  ) ).

  DATA(ls_customer) = get_customer_patterns( iv_customer ).
  RETURN lo_flow->exec( REF #( ls_customer ) ).
ENDMETHOD.

Result: SAP data → AI analysis → Business recommendations in 30 seconds.

Load Balancing: Right Model, Right Cost

" Smart routing based on complexity
DATA(lo_llm_smart) = zcl_llm_00_llm_lazy_composite=>new(
  io_llm       = lo_llm_mini     " Fast, cheap model
  io_llm_exp   = lo_llm_maxi     " Powerful, expensive model 
  iv_threshold = 1000            " Token threshold
).

" Simple queries → Mini model (fast + cheap)
" Complex queries → Maxi model (powerful + expensive)

 

 

PREDICTOKEN: The Secret Weapon

" Predict tokens before expensive API calls
DATA(lv_predicted_tokens) = zcl_llm_00_predictoken=>predict_tokens_gpt4( lv_text ).

IF lv_predicted_tokens < 500.
  lo_llm = lo_llm_mini.     " Cheap model
ELSEIF lv_predicted_tokens > 8000.
  lo_flow = lo_split_flow.  " Split into multiple calls
ENDIF.

Accuracy: 99.7% token prediction (R² > 0.997 on 50,000+ validation samples) without API calls.

One-Click Invoice Intelligence

Complete invoice processing workflow:

DATA(lo_flow) = zcl_llm_00_flow_lazy=>new( VALUE #(
  ( lo_extract_invoice_data )     
  ( lo_validate_against_po )      
  ( lo_flag_anomalies )          
  ( lo_generate_approval_routing )
  ( lo_update_workflow_system )  
) ).

" Process 1000 invoices in parallel
DATA(lo_parallel) = zcl_llm_00_step_lazy_parallel=>new(
  io_step = lo_flow->to_step( )
  io_llm  = lo_llm
).

Getting Started: The 10-Minute Setup

 

  1. Run: ZLLM_00_ONBOARD - Configure your LLM endpoints
  2. Demo: ZLLM_00_FLOW_DEMO - See chaining in action
  3. Build: Your first workflow in pure ABAP

 

DATA(lo_llm) = zcl_llm=>new( 'DEFAULT.ENV' ).
DATA(lo_step) = zcl_llm_00_step_lazy=>new_from_string(
  iv_usr = 'Analyze this data: {T}'
  io_llm = lo_llm
).
DATA(lr_result) = lo_step->exec( REF #( your_business_data ) ).

The "But What About..." Section

"What about Python's rich AI ecosystem?" Over half of enterprise AI work is data movement, not algorithms. ZLLM excels at the majority use case.

"What about scaling?" SAP systems already handle your enterprise load. ZLLM scales with your existing infrastructure.

"What about vendor lock-in?" ZLLM actually reduces vendor lock-in. You can hot-swap your LLM including self-hosted Ollama - try this with Joule 😃

# Monday: Local Ollama (zero vendor dependency)
API_MODEL=codellama
API_URL=http://localhost:11434/
API_KEY=ollama

# Tuesday: Switch to Azure (same ABAP code)
API_AZURE_FULL_URL=https://your-domain.openai.azure.com/...
API_KEY=your-azure-key

# Wednesday: Your own inference cluster
API_MODEL=your-custom-model
API_URL=http://your-internal-cluster/v1/
API_KEY=internal

One framework, any LLM provider. Change your .env file, restart your program. That's it.

Meanwhile, Joule ties you to whatever models SAP decides to support. ZLLM works with anything that speaks OpenAI API - including models that don't exist yet.

Why This Changes Everything

Before ZLLM: AI projects required new infrastructure, teams, security reviews, integration patterns, deployment processes.

After ZLLM: AI projects are ABAP development projects. Use existing everything.

The "cold start problem" disappears because there's no cold start. Your AI capabilities live where your business logic lives.

The Enterprise AI Future

This isn't another AI framework. This is enterprise AI that makes business sense:

 

  • ✅No integration projects - AI lives in your business logic
  • ✅No new infrastructure - Use your existing SAP systems
  • ✅No new security model - Inherit SAP's proven security
  • ✅No new deployment process - Deploy like any ABAP development

 

AGI* has been achieved. Now your ABAP can generate intelligence.

Ready to eliminate your AI integration hell?

⭐Star & Fork on GitHub - Get early access to the framework

Comment below: What's your most painful SAP business process? Let's see how ZLLM can eliminate that pain.

Build in hours what took quarters—or never shipped—six months ago.

The ideal enterprise AI integration is one that doesn't exist because the capability is native: "we already have an LLM at home!"

About the Author:

Alice Vinogradova is a Senior Software Engineer with two decades of ABAP/SAP experience and a passion for practical AI integration. She specializes in eliminating complexity through innovation, bringing cutting-edge AI capabilities to conservative enterprise environments without the traditional integration overhead.

P.S. This framework exists because I got tired of explaining why enterprise AI projects fail. Better to build the solution than argue about the problem. ^_^

*ABAP Generative Intelligence 😃

**No external API, except LLM of course.

Hashtags: #SAP #ABAP #AI #EnterpriseAI #LLM #Innovation #TechLeadership #LangChain #ZLLM

N.B. It was just released and Lars Hvam already sent 5 pull-requests with bug fixes! Thanks a mil, Lars ^_^

entry.title: New Custom Heuristic for Campaign Planning in PP/DS based on Order Quantity Thresholds
entry.content:
Summary

This blog post describes how to create a new custom heuristic for automatic creation of production campaigns from neighboring orders that have the same setup group, for specific customer business case where a minimum and a maximum number of units per campaign must be considered.

The target audience for this blog post will be consulting people, project team members, key users, and subject matter experts who are interested on the heuristic algorithms for PP/DS.

 

Introduction

In Production Planning and Detailed Scheduling (PP/DS), production campaign planning allows businesses to group related production orders together for more efficient production scheduling and cost allocation. This is particularly useful in process industries where production lines may require setup or cleaning between processing different production batches or before downtimes. By grouping orders into campaigns, you can optimize the sequence of operations, reduce setup costs, and potentially lower storage costs. 

You mainly use production campaigns in companies that manufacture their products on production lines. Campaign planning functions can combine orders for similar or identical products into production campaigns. This consolidation help reduces setup and storage costs significantly, allowing products to be manufactured in a single manufacturing process over a longer period.

You can decide whether you want to use campaign optimization to plan campaigns or campaign heuristics. If you want to use campaign optimization for your planning, the following requirements must be met:

  • You use only one bottleneck resource within a production data structure model or, if there are more bottleneck resources, they are defined as alternative resources only.
  • There are no cross-order relationships or pegging relationships between the activities at the bottleneck resource.

If these requirements are not met, then you can use campaign heuristics to plan campaigns automatically.

 

Campaign Planning

Campaign planning can utilize either campaign optimization or campaign heuristics. You can plan campaigns in PP/DS as follows:

  • Automatically
    • By using campaign optimization, in which the system creates campaigns. The system tries to find an optimum number and sequence of orders in a campaign, as well as the sequence between the campaigns, while taking setup and storage costs into account.
    • By processing the campaign heuristics in sequence.
  • Manually. You can use the detailed scheduling planning board or campaign transaction to determine the number and sequence of orders in a campaign, as well as the sequence between the campaigns.

During scheduling, the system checks the following campaign-specific requirements for those resources you specified as campaign-relevant:

  • The activities of a campaign cannot be interrupted by activities that do not belong to orders of the same campaign.
  • You can position an order at the beginning or end of a campaign. Setup orders, for example, are always at the beginning of a campaign, and cleaning orders are always at the end.

The system schedules the orders at these resources so above requirements are not violated. Furthermore, the minimum resource commitment is also considered, which means that when scheduling campaigns and their orders, the system tries to occupy as few resources as possible. If you use alternative modes, all activities of a campaign must take place at the same resource.

The system only checks the above campaign requirements for the resources if the following indicators are set:

  • The “Campaign-Relevant” indicator in the resource master.
  • The “Consider Campaign Requirements” indicator in the strategy profile.

Figure 1: “Campaign-Relevant” indicator in the resource master.

Figure 2: “Consider Campaign Requirements” indicator in the strategy profile.

 

Main Tools and Features for Campaign Planning

The main tools and features to manage campaign planning are:

  1. Detailed Scheduling Planning Board: transaction /SAPAPO/CDPS0 provides a graphical view of campaigns and orders, allowing you to plan and schedule campaigns visually.
  2. Campaign transaction: transaction /SAPAPO/PCMT provides a table-based view of campaigns, orders, and dates, offering a more structured approach to campaign planning.
  3. Campaign heuristics: a feature that can be used to optimize the planning of campaigns by automatically generating a finite production plan.

By using these tools, the following campaign functions among others are available:

  • Create campaigns.
  • Dissolve campaigns.
  • Remove orders from campaign.
  • Add orders into existing campaign.
  • Splitting campaigns.
  • Merging campaigns.
  • Move orders from one campaign to another.
  • Create cleaning/setup orders.

Figure 3: Visual representation of a campaign in the detailed scheduling planning board.

Figure 4: Table-based view of a campaign in the campaign transaction.

Figure 5: Example of campaign heuristics functions in the detailed scheduling planning board.

Campaigns are visually represented by bars above the operation bars, covering the operations within the campaign. You can use drag and drop to move the campaign bars on the time axis. The planning functions of the detailed scheduling planning board help you to schedule campaigns. By default, the campaign bar label includes the campaign number, description, and status. You can also access the PP/DS campaign number when using the product view (transaction /SAPAPO/RRP3).

The campaign number comes from an internal number range defined on transaction PCMNO. When saving the campaign, an external campaign number is also assigned containing the S/4HANA campaign number, from a number range defined on transaction CPCN:

Figure 6: Example of internal and external campaign number in PP/DS campaign transaction.

Figure 7: Example of external campaign number in S/4HANA campaign transaction.

When manually creating a campaign, you can select its color from a designated palette of campaign colors (six colors available). When automatically planning campaigns, the system automatically assigns the color specified in the campaign profile. The campaign profiles can be managed either through transaction /SAPAPO/PCMP directly or through the following customizing path: IMG -> Advanced Planning -> Production Campaign Planning --> Maintain Campaign Profile.

Figure 8: Palette of available colors for campaigns visualization.

In the campaign profile, you perform settings which the system uses to create campaigns and for campaign optimization. You create a campaign profile for a certain location and setup group. The “Maximum Number of Charges in a Campaign” specifies the maximum number of orders possible per campaign when using the setup group entered. The “Campaign Color” specifies the display color of the bars that represent the production campaign in the resource chart of the detailed scheduling planning board. The default color for campaigns is violet, which is the one used by default if not campaign profile found by setup group.

Figure 9: Example of settings on the campaign profile.

This automatic coloring based on the campaign profiles will only work for automatic campaigns creation. If users are going to create campaigns manually, then they must enter the color manually or default color will be applied. This means that campaign profiles do not apply for manually created campaigns.

Every production campaign in PP/DS has a status that documents the campaign’s current processing status. The campaign status is derived from the status of the orders contained in the PP/DS campaign:

  • Status Created: The campaign either contains no orders or only orders that have neither been released nor started.
  • Status Released: The campaign contains at least one order that has been released and no order that has been started.
  • Status Started: The campaign contains at least one order that has been started.

The campaign status is also displayed graphically at the campaign bar in the detailed scheduling planning board. The status is indicated by various hatch patterns in the campaign bar.

  • Status Created: Colored, no hatch.
  • Status Released: Colored, diagonal stripe.
  • Status Started: Colored, diagonal crosshatch.

  

Figure 10: Example of campaign bar display on the base of the production campaign status.

 

Standard Heuristics for Production Campaigns

A heuristic is a planning function that executes planning for selected objects using a particular algorithm. In this way, the focus of planning can be products, orders, operations, resources, or line networks. Several standard heuristics are available for campaign planning. You can use them in the production planning run or interactively in the detailed scheduling planning board to process production campaigns. The available heuristics for campaign planning are the following:

  1. Create production campaigns (algorithm SAP_PCM_CRT, function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_CREATE)

Using this heuristic, you create production campaigns from neighboring orders that manufacture the same product or have the same setup group, depending on the heuristic setting.

  1. Dissolve production campaigns (algorithm SAP_PCM_DIS, function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_DISSOLVE)

Using this heuristic, you delete production campaigns, for example, before you let PP/DS optimization process your plan. The algorithm deletes as many orders as possible from all campaigns with the selected properties, and then deletes the campaigns that do not contain any orders.

  1. Delete setup/clean-out orders (algorithm SAP_PCM_ODEL, function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_ORDERS_DELETE)

Using this heuristic, you delete production setup/clean-out orders, for example, before you let PP/DS optimization process your plan. The sequence-dependent setup activity at the beginning of the campaign that had duration 0 as the successor of the setup/clean-out order increases by the value defined in the setup matrix for the new sequence.

  1. Adjust setup/clean-out orders (algorithm SAP_PCM_SRVA, function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_SERVICE_ADAPT)

Using this heuristic, you create setup/clean-out orders for a campaign. The algorithm deletes all existing setup/clean-out orders and then replaces all existing sequence-dependent setup activities at the beginning of a campaign with a setup/clean-out order.

To use campaign heuristics in the detailed scheduling planning board, you must define a heuristic profile that contains the corresponding campaign heuristics and assign it to the overall profile of the detailed scheduling planning board. This is done through the following customizing path: IMG -> Advanced Planning -> Heuristics -> Maintain Heuristic Profiles.

Figure 11: Example of heuristic profile containing several campaign heuristics.

To call up a heuristic in the detailed scheduling planning board, you select the relevant objects and choose menu option “Functions” > “Heuristics”. To use the heuristic in the production planning run, you specify the processing steps you want to carry out and select the relevant objects. All the campaign heuristic algorithms consider the settings made in the campaign profile and in the definition of the heuristic.

When you define a heuristic, you first select the algorithm for the heuristic and second define the settings for the heuristic. This is done either through transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSC11 directly or through the following customizing path: IMG -> Advanced Planning -> Heuristics -> Maintain Heuristics.

Figure 12: Example of default heuristic settings for heuristic “Create production campaigns”.

To change the default settings relevant to the heuristic, you choose Settings for Heuristic in the detailed scheduling planning board.

Figure 13: Example of heuristic settings maintained on detailed scheduling planning board.

The list with the detailed steps of the overall campaign planning flow with campaign heuristics would the following:

 

Step

Description

1

Planning orders are created by MRP Live.

2

You delete existing campaigns, setup and clean-out orders using the heuristics “Dissolve campaign” and “Delete setup/clean-out orders” since PP/DS optimization cannot process them.

3

You start PP/DS optimization for selected resources (bottleneck resources) in the detailed scheduling planning board or the production planning run.

4

PP/DS optimization creates an order sequence with optimized setup times at the selected resources. Storage costs cannot be considered.

5

Using the heuristic “Create production campaigns”, you let the system create campaigns consisting of orders that follow each other and have the same setup group or product in the detailed scheduling planning board or the production planning run.

6

If you want to use setup or clean-out orders, you execute the heuristic “Adjust setup/clean-out orders” or the function “Adjust setup/clean-out orders” in the detailed scheduling planning board. The system replaces sequence-dependent setup activities by setup or clean-out orders.

7

If required, you correct the planning in the detailed scheduling planning board or the campaign transaction and eliminate campaign alerts . You can assign additional orders to campaign.

8

You fix campaigns if you do not want them to be changed by another automatic planning run. You freeze the scheduling result.

9

If the planning situation changes, you start again with step 2.

10

You convert the planning to process orders for SAP S/4HANA.

 

New custom heuristic for creation of production campaigns based on quantity thresholds

The standard algorithm SAP_PCM_CRT for creation of production campaigns (function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_CREATE) creates new production campaigns and assigns orders to them. The algorithm assumes that you use finite scheduling and the procedure for activity/order assignation and sequencing is the following:

  1. The algorithm chronologically sorts all activities on the selected campaign-relevant resources in the planning period, defined by the selected time profile.
  2. The algorithm creates production campaigns from all neighboring activities with the following requirements:
    • The activities are not already part of a production campaign.
    • The activities are not fixed.
    • The activities are not deallocated.
    • The activities have the same setup group or produce the same product, depending on the settings for the heuristic.

The algorithm also takes the settings defined in the campaign profile into consideration. For instance, if for certain location and setup group a campaign profile exists, only the defined maximum number of orders possible per campaign are allowed. You can also use the campaign requirement indicator in the settings for the heuristic to determine if campaigns can be interrupted by downtimes, for example.

Customer business case was that they did not want to restrict a maximum number of orders possible per campaign, but they needed to define a minimum and a maximum number of units per campaign. This units will be in the production unit of measure of the orders. The required criteria for campaign aggrupation were the same setup group.

SAP provides a lot of standard heuristics for planning in PP/DS, but also have an option to create your own custom heuristics either through transaction /SAPAPO/CDPSC11 directly or else through the following customizing path IMG -> Advanced Planning -> Heuristics -> Maintain Heuristics.

When creating the new custom heuristic, first step is to select the algorithm for the heuristic. The way to proceed is to copy the standard function module /SAPAPO/HEUR_PCM_CREATE into a custom function module containing the own logic and copy other auxiliar function modules in case custom changes are also required.

Second step is to define the settings for the heuristic. In our business case, the two parameters containing the “Minimum Campaign Units” and the “Maximum Campaign Units” were added. Furthermore, the corresponding “Unit of measure” for these two parameters was also added, to avoid issues with production units different than base units of measure, and to be valid for other scenarios.

Figure 14: Example of default heuristic settings for custom heuristic algorithm.

Figure 15: Example of custom heuristic settings maintained on detailed scheduling planning board.

As customer did not want to restrict a maximum number of orders possible per campaign, this parameter was leave it on 0 (no restrictions) on the customizing campaign profiles. The required changes were implemented on business logic on the new custom heuristic to consider minimum and maximum number of units per campaign.

If you would like to work with your own heuristics in the detailed scheduling planning board, two pre-requisites must be fulfilled to allow custom heuristics for execution:

  • The new custom heuristic must be contained on the heuristic profile assigned to the overall profile of the detailed scheduling planning board. Then, the new heuristic will appear in the list of available heuristics in the menu of interactive planning.
  • The new custom heuristic must also be maintained on the scope profile assigned to the material on the “Advanced Planning” material master view, when the “Scope Limitation” is selected as ‘PP/DS Restricted for Inhouse Only’ or ‘PP/DS Restricted’.

The heuristics algorithms other than from the above lists are not allowed.

Figure 16: Example of scope profile on “Advanced Planning” material master view.

Figure 17: Example of custom heuristic algorithm allowed on scope profile.

The standard campaign text is the description of the leading material or a fixed text in case of empty description. Customer requested the main key of the resource code (work center code) to be placed on the campaign text. This was directly coded on the new custom logic by overwriting the standard code, but it is also possible to achieve by using BADI “/SAPAPO/PCM01_TXTOPT”.

Figure 18: Example of production campaign text containing the resource key.

The planning log in PP/DS contains messages that the system issues during interactive planning or in background planning. You can use the planning log to analyze planning problems and identify the causes of the problems. On the “Settings” for the “Planning Log” on the detailed scheduling planning board you can select which types of messages you want to display on the planning log, such as termination messages or warnings or all messages.

Figure 19: Example of settings for the planning log on the detailed scheduling planning board.

Review and analysis of heuristics logs is an important activity which helps planners rectify many errors and make sure that planning results are very accurate. Customer requested a more detailed planning log containing more messages relative to specific custom logic. This was directly coded on the new custom logic by adding the specific new control messages.

Figure 20: Example of custom messages displayed in the planning log on the detailed scheduling planning board.

 

When you run PP/DS heuristic in background, you can invoke transaction /SAPAPO/RRPLOG1 to display the planning log. For interactive planning, until you save, you cannot watch the log on transaction /SAPAPO/RRPLOG1.

Figure 21: Selection screen of transaction /SAPAPO/RRPLOG1 for displaying planning logs.

 

Helpful Links

SAP Online Help for Production Campaign Planning for PP/DS

Production Campaign Planning

Functions for Production Campaign Planning

Campaign Optimization

Heuristics for Campaign Planning

Campaign Alerts

 

[Disclaimer: Written by Juan Ventura. This blog post is written by the author based on his personal experience while working on customers with SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. Before implementing any solution, you are advised to always do the analysis and adopt a solution for specific business case only if it suits customer requirements and testing is successful.]

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Embedded Analytics for Finance: Apps, Architecture, and Implementation Tips
entry.content:
Introduction

If you come from the world of SAP R/3 or SAP ECC, you may hear the term ‘embedded analytics’ and assume it refers to a module or add-on, but it’s really a capability built into S/4HANA itself.

In this post, I’ll walk through embedded analytics, showing some examples for finance and sharing a few thoughts based on my own experience.

The old paradigm

SAP R/3 and SAP ECC are transactional systems. They sit on top of traditional relational databases that are not optimised for analytical operations. They couldn’t efficiently calculate totals on the fly, never mind aggregation, filtering, sorting etc.
